name: Vācaspati Miśra (Yoga commentator)
dates_notes: Conventionally dated c. 900–980 CE. The colophon of his *Nyāya-Sūcī-Nibandha* dates that work to Saṃvat 898 (= 841 CE on one reading, c. 976 CE on another reading depending on the era used); the consensus from Frauwallner 1959 and the standard secondary literature accepts the later reading and places Vācaspati in the second half of the tenth century. The entry treats the Yoga-commentarial aspect of his polymath corpus — the *Tattva-Vaiśāradī* — separately from his Advaita work (*Bhāmatī*) which is catalogued under `vacaspati.json`.
core_thesis: Vācaspati Miśra is the principal medieval polymath of the Sanskrit darśana tradition, commenting authoritatively on Advaita Vedānta (*Bhāmatī* on Śaṅkara's *Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya*), on Sāṅkhya (*Sāṅkhya-Tattva-Kaumudī* on Īśvarakṛṣṇa's *Sāṅkhya-Kārikā*), on Nyāya (*Nyāya-Vārttika-Tātparya-Ṭīkā* on Uddyotakara), on Mīmāṃsā (*Tattva-Bindu*), and on Yoga (*Tattva-Vaiśāradī* on Vyāsa's *Yoga-Bhāṣya*). The present entry treats his Yoga commentary; the Advaita work is catalogued separately under `vacaspati.json`. The *Tattva-Vaiśāradī* (Skill in the Tattvas) is a sub-commentary on Vyāsa's *Yoga-Bhāṣya* (the canonical commentary on Patañjali's *Yoga-Sūtra*) and is the principal medieval exegetical apparatus for the school. Vācaspati's distinctive contribution to Yoga is the systematic integration of the Yoga ontology — the standard twenty-five Sāṅkhya *tattvas* plus Īśvara — with the cognitive-phenomenological analysis of the path: the *citta-vṛtti-nirodha* (cessation of mental modifications) defining of yoga in *Yoga-Sūtra* 1.2 is articulated as the systematic cessation, in stages, of each of the five *vṛttis* (correct cognition, error, conceptual construction, sleep, memory; *YS* 1.6–11), with the meditative attainments (*samādhi*) classified as *samprajñāta* (with conceptual content) and *asamprajñāta* (without) on the basis of the residual presence or absence of *saṃskāras*. The commentary is the canonical mature articulation of the school and is the standard exegetical reference for the *Yoga-Sūtra* in the subsequent commentarial tradition. Vācaspati's Yoga commentary is distinct from his Advaita work in that he there takes the Yoga ontology seriously on its own terms — the *puruṣa-prakṛti* duality and Īśvara as a distinct *puruṣa* — rather than reading the Yoga material through the Advaita lens; the polymath holds the systems separately and articulates each in its own register.

engaged_works.title: Tattva-Vaisaradi
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored by Vācaspati Miśra. Sanskrit text extant in multiple editions (Ananandāśrama 1904, Banaras Sanskrit Series, Kashi Sanskrit Series). The standard English study is Woods 1914 (*The Yoga-System of Patañjali*, which translates Patañjali, Vyāsa, and Vācaspati together).

ascription_evidence.description: Standard Sanskrit darśana doxographical tradition; the colophon attribution is uniform across the manuscript witnesses.

engaged_works.summary: A complete sub-commentary on Vyāsa's *Yoga-Bhāṣya*, organized following the four *pādas* of the *Yoga-Sūtra*: *Samādhi-Pāda* (on the nature of yoga and the kinds of samādhi), *Sādhana-Pāda* (on the practical means: the eight-limbed yoga of *yama*, *niyama*, *āsana*, *prāṇāyāma*, *pratyāhāra*, *dhāraṇā*, *dhyāna*, *samādhi*), *Vibhūti-Pāda* (on the supernormal attainments produced by *saṃyama* — concentrated meditation — on various objects), and *Kaivalya-Pāda* (on the final isolation of *puruṣa* from *prakṛti* constituting liberation). The commentary supplies the technical apparatus for each of Vyāsa's positions and frequently engages with the rival Buddhist analysis (particularly the analysis of momentariness and the analysis of the *citta-stream*) and with the rival Mīmāṃsā analysis (particularly the analysis of the relation between Yoga and Vedic ritual). The treatise is the principal medieval exegetical apparatus for classical Yoga and is the standard reference for the subsequent tradition.

reception_notes: Vācaspati Miśra's Yoga commentary is the standard medieval reference for the *Yoga-Sūtra* and the principal source through which classical Yoga reaches the subsequent commentarial tradition (Vijñānabhikṣu's *Yoga-Vārttika*, Bhoja's *Rāja-Mārtaṇḍa*, the modern editions and translations). The treatise's integration with Vācaspati's parallel Sāṅkhya commentary (*Sāṅkhya-Tattva-Kaumudī*) supplies the canonical medieval articulation of the Sāṅkhya–Yoga doctrinal complex.
